hero.name: Tyrese Mosley
hero.subtitle: Information Science student building real tech, on track for a career in cybersecurity.

about.heading: Who I am
about.bio: My name is Tyrese Mosley, and I am an Information Science student at the University of Pittsburgh with a passion for creating a meaningful impact in the world. I enjoy transforming complex challenges into clean and intuitive experiences. I also believe it is essential to protect information and ensure safe digital interactions. My goal is to become a cybersecurity analyst in the near future.
about.journey: My journey started with a simple curiosity at a young age about how things work on the internet. That curiosity turned into learning as much as I could, eventually leading me to build real tools for a nonprofit, We Love Philly, in Philadelphia, and making real world experiences and connections. Every project teaches me something new, and every experience is a step toward the kind of career that I want.
about.footer: Philadelphia, PA · Posse Scholar · Information Science

quote.verse: Yet what we suffer now is nothing compared to the glory he will reveal to us later.

projects.title: We Love Philly Youth Tracker
projects.description: Internal student lifecycle tracking platform for a Philadelphia nonprofit. Replaces fragmented spreadsheets and Airtable with a single source of truth, tracking students from application through alumni engagement.

caseStudy.hook: A secure, fully-synced student tracking platform that brought 3 disconnected tools into one source of truth for 547+ Philadelphia youth students.
caseStudy.story:
We Love Philly tracks over 547 students across their entire journey, from application to alumni status, but that data lives scattered across Google Classroom, Airtable, and Google Sheets, with no single source of truth. Staff are having to manually cross-reference these three tools just to answer the basic question of how many students are currently active.

As Technology Project Lead, I owned this problem end to end — mapping out how each system stored and structured its data, then using AI-assisted development to architect and ship it. I designed the integration so that Airtable runs fully and is able to sync at 2: 00 a. m. every night. The Google Classroom syncs grades and attendance by matching students' emails at 4: 00 a. m. In Zapier, a Webhook handles the real-time updates from record changes outside the regular working hours. I also was able to design a self-serve flow so that when staff make any new Google Sheets for the cohort's attendance and hours, they will be able to connect seamlessly with the app.

Since this platform handles sensitive student data, I implemented a two-factor authentication that was restricted to verify We Love Philly staff only. Staff sign in with their email and password and then receive a six-digit code sent to their inbox that must be entered to complete the site sign-in, adding a second layer of protection beyond just credentials. This was my first-hand implication of two-factor authentication directly on a site.

Having this system gives the leadership a live breakdown of every student by stage, from applicant to enrolled to active to interning or alumni. They're all searchable and filtered across all 547 records. That design supports both scheduled and on-demand reporting so staff can generate a report when they need one or receive automated reports on a recurring schedule without lifting a finger. Also, both self-monitoring so that the team can see whether a sync has succeeded, was partially completed, or failed altogether, rather than them having to search and find anything that's wrong.

The hardest part of this wasn't writing the code, it was the system thinking. Mapping how the three tools with different data models, update frequencies, and ownership needed to all come together into one reliable source, securing access to the data appropriately, debugging issues as they surfaced in production, and working with an AI assistant to develop tools to translate my decisions into working software.

projects.title: WLP PTO Portal
projects.description: Internal PTO management system for nonprofit staff. Features request submission, manager approvals, team calendar with blackout dates, email notifications, and a GitHub-backed CMS.

projects.title: AWS Mini-SOC Incident Response Project
projects.description: I built a tiny AWS environment with logging turned on, deliberately created a weak spot (an over-permissioned user), simulated an attacker poking around, then investigated it like a SOC analyst would and wrote it up as a formal incident report.

caseStudy.hook: A simulated AWS cloud security incident, built, detected, investigated, and remediated using the same tools and framework real SOC analysts use on the job.
caseStudy.story:
Most cybersecurity students read about threat detection. This project was about actually doing it. I spun up a real AWS environment, deliberately introduced a misconfiguration, simulated attacker behavior, and investigated it the same way a SOC analyst would on their first day.

Before simulating anything, I stood up the detection infrastructure first. I enabled AWS CloudTrail to log every API call across all regions, and activated AWS GuardDuty for automated threat detection. This mirrors real SOC work: your monitoring has to be running before the incident, not after.

I then created a synthetic IAM user called dev-test-user and attached AdministratorAccess, giving it total unrestricted control over the entire AWS account. This represented the classic misconfiguration that shows up in real breaches: too much access given to the wrong identity.

Using the AWS CLI, I ran a series of reconnaissance commands as dev-test-user. GetCallerIdentity, ListUsers, ListBuckets, DescribeInstances, and DescribeSecurityGroups. This simulated an attacker who obtained leaked credentials and is quietly mapping out what they have access to. Each command was logged by CloudTrail with exact timestamps.

GuardDuty flagged the activity within minutes, raising a finding for unauthorized root account credential usage on sensitive API calls. I cross-referenced the GuardDuty finding against the CloudTrail event history filtered to dev-test-user, building a complete attack timeline from first API call to detection.

The impact assessment made the stakes clear. With AdministratorAccess active, a real attacker could have exfiltrated or destroyed all S3 data, terminated production servers, opened security group ports to the public internet, created persistent backdoor IAM users, disabled CloudTrail to cover their tracks, and locked legitimate admins out of the account entirely.

Remediation followed the NIST SP 800-61 incident response framework. I deactivated the access key, deleted the over-privileged user, disabled GuardDuty and CloudTrail after evidence collection, and confirmed zero billing impact at close. Root cause: violation of least privilege. Recommended fix: scoped IAM policies, mandatory MFA on all users, and key rotation schedules.
